Managing fake content, to me, is about using common sense and not getting too wrapped up in it all. With my high openness, I'm curious and willing to learn new things, so I try to fact-check by looking at reliable sources, like news from trusted outlets or asking my family, who are more tech-savvy. I think the best way to deal with it is to limit time online, focus on real-life connections like board games with friends or rock climbing, and rely on personal networks for truth. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As an 88-year-old plumber from Kansas City, I've seen a lot change over the years, and social media is one of those things that makes me anxious, especially with all the fake content floating around. I only check it a few times a month, mostly to see updates from my four kids or share a recipe from my cooking hobby, but even then, I stumble upon things that just don't seem right. Managing fake content, to me, is about using common sense and not getting too wrapped up in it all. With my high openness, I'm curious and willing to learn new things, so I try to fact-check by looking at reliable sources, like news from trusted outlets or asking my family, who are more tech-savvy. But my low conscientiousness means I'm not very organized or diligent about it—I might forget to verify something if I'm busy with work or enjoying classical music. My low agreeableness makes me skeptical; I don't trust everything I see online and often question motives, which helps me spot fakes. However, my high neuroticism means this anxiety can overwhelm me, making me avoid social media more than engage, which ironically reduces my exposure to fake content. I think the best way to deal with it is to limit time online, focus on real-life connections like board games with friends or rock climbing, and rely on personal networks for truth. Social media companies should do more, but as an older native American with a simple education, I believe in personal responsibility and staying grounded in what I know is real.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age 88 suggests life experience and caution; occupation as plumber implies practical, hands-on approach; low education level indicates reliance on common sense over formal training; anxious psychological feature and high neuroticism lead to avoidance and stress around fake content; high openness supports curiosity and learning but low conscientiousness results in inconsistent fact-checking; low agreeableness fosters skepticism; time on social media (few times monthly) limits exposure; hobbies like cooking and classical music emphasize real-world interests over digital engagement; widowed status with children suggests family as a trusted source; overall tone is cautious, practical, and somewhat dismissive of social media complexities.
response_parsed.tone: cautious, practical, skeptical, and somewhat dismissive, with a focus on personal experience and real-world connections.
